title: 1 Neuroinfectious Disease
text:
1 Neuroinfectious Disease
S
MENINGITIS vs. ENCEPHALITIS (start treatment empirically, don’t wait for LP…): Meningitis Encephalitis
S/S: HA, photo/phono/meningismus, N/V, fever, malaise, irritability S/S: similar to meningitis, plus acute confusion, personality changes, seizures
Bugs: Viral: echo, coxsackie, mumps, HSV-2, adeno Bacterial: by age… <1m: GBS, E.coli, Listeria (“hear bells”) 1-23m: S.pneumo, N.mening, H.flu, E.coli 2-50y: N.mening, S.pneumo 50+y: S.pneumo, N.mening, Listeria, GNR
Bugs: HSV-1 (#1), arbo (SLE, LAC, EEE, WEE), WNV, VZV, legionella
W/U: HCT  LP (routine, HSV PCR, t/c crypto/TB/AFB/viral studies) W/U: HCT  if no abscess  LP (routine, HSV PCR, t/c viral/toxo)  t/c MRI and EEG Treatment: empirically… Viral: supportive (if high suspicion for HSV, start acyclovir 10 mg/kg IV q8) Bacterial: <1m cefotax/ceftriax + ampicillin 1-23m cefotax or ceftriax + vanco 2-50y ceftriax + vanco 50+y ceftriax + vanco+ ampicillin Fungal: amphoB + flucytosine + fluconazole TB: rifampin+INH+pyrazinamide+ethambutol, +/- streptomycin (“RIPES”) * if trauma: use cefepime or meropenem * Dexamethasone 10 mg IV q6h x 4 days was shown to be beneficial in subgroups with pneumococcal meningitis, 1st dose 20 min before abx.
Treatment: by bug… HSV/VZV: acyclovir 10 mg/kg IV q8 x 14+d CMV: ganciclovir or foscarnet Legionella: azithro, quinolone, tatracyclines
Prognosis: - aseptic/viral: most improve over 2 weeks - bacterial: 20% mortality (S. pneumo worst)
Prognosis: HSV& EEE = 70% die untreated, 25% treated Others = 5-20% mortality rate
Tunkel AR et. al. Practice guidelines for the management of bacterial meningitis. Clin Infect Dis 2004 Nov 1;39(9):1267-84. de Gans et. Al. Dexamethasone in adults with bacterial meningitis. NEJM, 2002; 347(20): 1549-56.
Serum Studies 1. Viruses: Measure IgM and IgG antibodies acute and convalescent (4 fold rise in IgG): St. Louis, West Nile, Eastern and Western Equine, Japanese encephalitis, Dengue fever EBV (VCA IgM and IgG and EBNA), VZV, HSV-1 (IgM), Rabies, HIV (RNA can be checked early when viral load high but ELISA weakly positive or negative) 2. Tick-borne bacteria: IgG & IgM by indirect immunofluorescence for RMSF, Lyme ELISA- confirmatory Western blot, Ehrlichia antibodies by indirect fluorescent antibody 3. Parasites: Toxoplasma gondii - No IgG or IgM Abs does not exclude toxoplasma encephalitis in AIDS patients - If +ve in HIV patient, tx with pyrimethamine/sulfadiazine and folinic acid. (Incidence reduced among people receiving prophylaxis with sulfadiazine or dapsone and pyrimethamine against Pneumocystis jiroveci pneumonia) b) If toxoplasma serology negative, check CSF EBV pcr and consider brain biopsy of any accessible mass lesion for lymphoma Taenia solium (cysticercosis): ELISA, but 50% with solitary lesions will be seronegative

title: Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome, Serotonin Syndrome, and Acute Dystonia
text: NEUROLEPTIC MALIGNANT SYNDROME (NMS) Signs and symptoms 1. Mental status change: agitated delirium, confusion, encephalopathy (82%) 2. Muscular rigidity: “lead pipe” tremor (up to 92%)  elevated CPK and myoglobinuric acute renal failure 3. Hyperthermia: >38°C (87%), >40°C (40%) 4. Autonomic instability: tachycardia (88%), labile BP (77%), tachypnea (73%), diaphoresis Diagnosis 1. Exclude other etiologies: acute drug intoxication, metabolic derangements, meningitis, etc. Treatment 1. Admit to ICU 2. Discontinue offending agent (e.g. neuroleptics, D2 blocking antiemetics or abrupt w/d levodopa) 3. Supportive care for Autonomic instability, DVT/PE prophylaxis, IVFs to prevent rhabdo 4. Symptomatic treatment: a. Agitation - Lorazepam 0.5-1mg prn (may also help with rigidity) b. Rigidity – Dantrolene 0.25-2mg/kg IV q6-12 hours (max dose 10mg/kg/day; be aware of hepatotoxicity) c. Fever – cooling blankets, ice lavage, acetaminophen SEROTONIN SYNDROME (SS) Signs and symptoms 1. Mental status change: agitated delirium, restlessness, disorientation 2. Neuromuscular hyperreactivity: tremor, rigidity, myoclonus, rigidity, hyperreflexia (+Babinski and clonus) 3. Autonomic instability: dilated pupils, diaphoresis, hypertension, diarrhea, vomiting Diagnosis 1. Exclude other etiologies: acute drug intoxication, metabolic derangements, meningitis, etc. Treatment 1. Admit to ICU 2. Discontinue offending agent (e.g. SSRIs, MAOIs, buspirone, trazodone, lithium, opiates, etc.) 3. Supportive care for Autonomic instability, DVT/PE prophylaxis 4. Symptomatic treatment: a. Agitation, Neuromuscular hyperreactivity - Lorazepam 0.5-1mg prn b. Cyptoheptadine (only for severe cases) 12 mg PO, then 2mg q2 hours until clinical response ACUTE DYSTONIC REACTION Signs and symptoms 1. Intermittent or sustained dystonic posturing due to blockade of D2 receptor (neuroleptics, certain antiemetics) Diagnosis 1. Review medication list/MAR for offending agent. 2. Exclude metabolic derangements (hypomagnesemia, hypocalcemia) or other drug toxicity. Treatment 1. Discontinue offending agent 2. Diphenhydramine 25-50mg IV/IM vs Lorazepam 0.5-1mg IV (acute); Diphenhydramine 25mg PO qid (x48hr) 3. Alternative therapy: Benztropine 2mg IV/IM (acute); Benztropine 1-2mg PO bid (x48hr)

title: 1 Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome, Serotonin Syndrome, and Acute Dystonia
text:
1 Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome, Serotonin Syndrome, and Acute Dystonia
NEUROLEPTIC MALIGNANT SYNDROME (NMS)
Signs and symptoms 1. Mental status change: agitated delirium, confusion, encephalopathy (82%) 2. Muscular rigidity: “lead pipe” tremor (up to 92%)  elevated CPK and myoglobinuric acute renal failure 3. Hyperthermia: >38°C (87%), >40°C (40%) 4. Autonomic instability: tachycardia (88%), labile BP (77%), tachypnea (73%), diaphoresis
Diagnosis 1. Exclude other etiologies: acute drug intoxication, metabolic derangements, meningitis, etc.
Treatment 1. Admit to ICU 2. Discontinue offending agent (e.g. neuroleptics, D2 blocking antiemetics or abrupt w/d levodopa) 3. Supportive care for Autonomic instability, DVT/PE prophylaxis, IVFs to prevent rhabdo 4. Symptomatic treatment: a. Agitation - Lorazepam 0.5-1mg prn (may also help with rigidity) b. Rigidity – Dantrolene 0.25-2mg/kg IV q6-12 hours (max dose 10mg/kg/day; be aware of hepatotoxicity) c. Fever – cooling blankets, ice lavage, acetaminophen
SEROTONIN SYNDROME (SS)
Signs and symptoms 1. Mental status change: agitated delirium, restlessness, disorientation 2. Neuromuscular hyperreactivity: tremor, rigidity, myoclonus, rigidity, hyperreflexia (+Babinski and clonus) 3. Autonomic instability: dilated pupils, diaphoresis, hypertension, diarrhea, vomiting
Diagnosis 1. Exclude other etiologies: acute drug intoxication, metabolic derangements, meningitis, etc.
Treatment 1. Admit to ICU 2. Discontinue offending agent (e.g. SSRIs, MAOIs, buspirone, trazodone, lithium, opiates, etc.) 3. Supportive care for Autonomic instability, DVT/PE prophylaxis 4. Symptomatic treatment:

Agitation, Neuromuscular hyperreactivity


Lorazepam 0.5-1mg prn b. Cyptoheptadine (only for severe cases) 12 mg PO, then 2mg q2 hours until clinical response

ACUTE DYSTONIC REACTION
Signs and symptoms 1. Intermittent or sustained dystonic posturing due to blockade of D2 receptor (neuroleptics, certain antiemetics)
Diagnosis 1. Review medication list/MAR for offending agent. 2. Exclude metabolic derangements (hypomagnesemia, hypocalcemia) or other drug toxicity.
Treatment 1. Discontinue offending agent 2. Diphenhydramine 25-50mg IV/IM vs Lorazepam 0.5-1mg IV (acute); Diphenhydramine 25mg PO qid (x48hr) 3. Alternative therapy: Benztropine 2mg IV/IM (acute); Benztropine 1-2mg PO bid (x48hr)
Neuro-oncology Emergencies
Common Medical Complications of Brain Tumors
Brain tumor
Direct effects - Epilepsy - Stroke - SIADH - Diabetes insipidus - Behavioral symptoms
Indirect effects - VTE - Stroke
Iatrogenic causes
Surgery - Stroke - Cerebral abscess
Chemotherapy - Nausea, vomiting - Constipation - HTN - Stroke - PRES - Bowel perforation - Myelotoxicity
Radiation - Fatigue - Dermatitis - Memory loss - Hearing loss - Cataracts
Steroids - Cushing syndrome - Infection - Diabetes mellitus - Mood disorder - Myopathy - Tremor
AEDs - Sedation - Fatigue - Irritability - Hyponatremia - Tremor - Rash - Myelotoxicity
Antineoplastic therapies Supportive therapies
Mass effect
Neuro- onc specifics

Vasogenic edema: tumor increases vascular permeability responds to steroids
If new lesion and suspicious for CNS lymphoma, consider osmotic therapy as a bridge to urgent biopsy as dexamethasone can decrease yield of biopsy

Acute tx

Use lowest effective dose of dexamethasone: typically 4 mg BID sufficient – attempt to taper to lowest dose that does not cause return of substantive deficits that impair function
Schedule at 6 am and 2 pm to avoid insomnia

Other - Select patients (ie. large contrast enhancing tumors with significant vasogenic edema) may benefit from bevacizumab long term as can improve edema over days to weeks
Herniation Acute tx - If obtunded or herniation: Dexamethasone 10 mg IV once, then 4 mg q6h - If need urgent decompression, consider bridging with osmotic therapy (see herniation section for further details – call herniation phone)
Hydro- cephalus
Neuro- onc specifics

Communicating hydrocephalus: common w/ leptomeningeal carcinomatosis
Noncommunicating hydrocephalus: often from structural lesion obstructing ventricular system Acute tx
May need EVD/VPS (page neurosurgery)

Epidural spinal cord compression
Acute tx - If neurologic deficits or pain associated with compression: immediate treatment with high dose IV dexamethasone (10 mg once, then 4 mg q6h) - Definitive treatment: surgical decompression (page spine on call) Other - May require radiation for treatment or palliation
Seizures and status epilepticus
Neuro- onc specifics

Tend to be focal, but can rapidly generalize
Risk factors: medication, metabolic derangement, paraneoplastic encephalitis, infection, PRES
No benefit from seizure prophylaxis

Acute tx - If status epilepticus: treat as needed as terminating seizure activity may outweigh less immediate risk of drug interaction Other - See AED toxicities table for further details
Pituitary Apoplexy Acute tx - Involve endocrinology ASAP - Supportive measures, hydrocortisone 100 mg IV (prevent adrenal crisis)
PRES
Neuro- onc specifics

Associated with bevacizumab, sorafenib, cyclophosphamide, l-asparaginase, cisplatin, gemcitabine; immunosuppressants such as tacrolimus and high dose steroids; and consequence of uncontrolled hypertension Acute tx
Management of blood pressure

Infection risk
Neuro- onc specifics

Meningitis: often does not have classic triad and without CSF pleocytosis in cancer patients
Common causative organisms: staph aureus, coag negative staph
In immunocompromised: also consider fungal (crypto, blastomycosis, histoplasmosis) and parasitic (toxoplasmosis)
Viral encephalitis more common with immunosuppression: HSV, VZV (consider antibody IgG index testing), CMV, HHV6, WNV, JC virus (PML)
Pneumocystis jiroveci: at risk due to lymphopenia from TMZ or steroids
Candida (thrush) common due to immunosuppression
At risk for cerebral abscess from neurosurgical interventions in context of steroid use

Acute tx - For meningitis coverage: if in hospital or undergone a neurosurgical procedure, in addition to vancomycin, include cefepime (vs ceftazidime or meropenem) rather than ceftriaxone
Other - If chronic ALC <500 or chronic steroids: consider daily bactrim SS and acyclovir 400mg daily as prophylaxis
Radiation necrosis
Neuro- onc specifics - More likely to occur with doses of radiation >50 Gy
Acute tx - Treatment: Dexamethasone 4-24 mg in divided doses to reduce mass effect Other - If cannot tolerate steroids, bevacizumab is an alternative
Thrombo- embolic events
Neuro- onc specifics

High risk for ischemic stroke, pulmonary embolism, DVT, most commonly in postoperative setting and subsequent 6 months
Hypercoagulable state
Pro-thrombotic therapies: bevacizumab, sunitinib, sorafenib, cisplatin Acute tx
Anticoagulation unless contraindication present

Ischemic stroke
Neuro- onc specifics

Chemotherapy: promotes risk of stroke with immune system compromise – bacterial endocarditis, sepsis, VZV vasculopathy
Increased risk in cancer population from: hypercoagulable, prothombotic therapies, radiation to head and neck (accelerated atherosclerosis / vasculopathy – can cause moyamoya), tumor emboli from cardiac metastasis, arterial occlusion by tumor, PRES

Acute tx - IV tPA relatively contraindicated - Stop bevacizumab - Otherwise, management per acute ischemic stroke pathway
Intra- parenchymal hemorrhage
Neuro- onc specifics

Increased risk from chemotherapy, hematologic malignancies (thrombocytopenia, coagulopathy), bevacizumab, radiation vasculopathy, and brain tumors themselves can bleed spontaneously
Most prone to hemorrhage
of primary CNS tumors: oligodendroglioma
of CNS metastases: melanoma, lung, papillary thyroid, renal cell cancer, choriocarcinoma
high risk hematologic malignancies: APML

Acute tx - No different than acute IPH management with blood pressure monitoring, consideration of EVD/VPS if evidence of hydrocephalus, or decompression if evidence of herniation
Other - If suspicious IPH suggestive of underlying CNS neoplasm: repeat MRI 6-12 weeks after initial hemorrhage
Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis
Neuro- onc specifics

Increased risk from hypercoagulable state, treatment of cancer can lead to severe dehydration, certain medications can trigger thrombosis (tamoxifen, l-asparaginase, heparin (especially if HIT)), venous compression by extrinsic metastasis, bacterial infection (meningitis or ear infection) Acute tx
Anticoagulation unless contraindication present

Para- neoplastic syndrome
Neuro- onc specifics

See paraneoplastic table for details on specific antibodies and related neurologic syndromes

Acute tx

Immunosuppression: high dose steroids (ie. 1 g IV methylprednisolone for 5 days)
IVIG 2g/kg divided over 2-5 days
Plasma exchange

Other - Treat underlying cancer - If refractory to acute therapy: consider rituximab, cyclophosphamide, tacrolimus, cyclosporine
Adapted from Lin AL et al. Neurologic Emergencies in the Cancer Patient: Diagnosis and Management. J Intensive Care Med. 2017 Feb; 32(2): 99–115. and Mohile NA. Medical Complications of Brain Tumors. Continuum (Minneap Minn). 2017 Dec;23(6, Neuro-oncology):1635-1652.

title: 1 Neurologic Examination
text:
1 Neurologic Examination
THE NEUROLOGICAL EXAMINATION
Mental Status
Arousal state: Alert, Sleepy, Lethargic, Stuporous, Coma. Best to describe (e.g. opens eyes spontaneously vs tovoice vs sternal rub vs deep pain) Attention: spell WORLD backwards, months backwards, serial 7’s, count from 20 to 1 Language: Fluency, repetition, naming, reading, writing, comprehension (1-step midline vs appendicular commands vs 2-step vs 2-step reversed grammar (touch your nose after pointing to the ceiling) Fund of Knowledge- current events, quality of medical Hx Praxis: Show me how you use a hammer Memory: 3-5 objects at 1 minute, digit span (avg 7), story retelling. Distant memory. Visuospatial: Organization of space (clock drawing, complex figure copy) Neglect- extinction, line bisection, target elimination Executive: Organization of knowledge, inhibition, sequencing, perseveration Verbal fluency: name animals in 1 minute (>20 is nml) Oral trails (A1, B2, 3C….), Luria 3-step (fist, edge, palm) registration and repeat 3 times. Go/no go (e.g. raise your hand every time I say a letter, except when I say ‘D’) Cranial Nerves Fundus—disc/vasculature/venous pulsations/retina Visual acuity (pinhole to correct refraction), color vision (especially red desaturation) Visual fields (cut/neglect) Pupils- size, reactivity, ptosis Extraocular mm- saccades and smooth pursuit (ductions-both eyes, versions-one eye, alignment) Face sensation—LT/PP/temp, v1-v3 dist, corneal reflex Face strength— central vs peripheral, hyperacusis, dysgusia, corneal dehydration Hearing— hi/lo pitch, Weber, Rinne. Finger rub is usually enough. Vestibular- VOR, past pointing, Fukuda march test, Dix-Hallpike, nystagmus Palate elevation—Aaaah, gag, uvula position, myoclonus (CTT lesion) SCM strength/bulk—innervates ipsi SCM, contra trap Tongue—position, bulk, fasciculations, strength (tongue against cheek) Motor Bulk (atrophy, fasciculations) Tone (flaccid, spastic/clasp knife, rigid/lead pipe), abnl mvmt (freq, amp, rest, action, tremor) Strength: Drift, satelliting, isolated muscles (3=antigravity), functional tests- heel/toe walking, rise from chair with arms crossed, squat, deep knee bend Sensory Extremities/trunk/back/perineum. Look for a sensory level; Romberg Anterolateral system (temp/pp/some ft) Dorsal Columns (lt/vib/discrimination/proprioception/some ft) Cortical sensory signs- graphasthesia, extinction, stereoagnosia Coordination Midline/vermis: trunk/hemispheres: appendicular coordination FTN, HTS, titubation, optic ataxia, nystagmus, rhythm testing (RAM) Reflexes DTRs: 0 to 4+: 3= spread of reflex, not amplitude of response, Jaw jerk (to distinguish cord/brain) Frontal release (glabellar, rooting, snouting, palmomental, grasp) Hoffman/Babinski, clonus Gait Base of stance, Stability of stance (provocation), posture, Initiation of gait, Stepping, Base of gait, Arm swing, Turning strength and balance–tandem walk

title: 1 Neurotology
text:
1 Neurotology
THE DIZZY PATIENT
Patients use the term dizziness to describe a wide variety of symptoms: vertigo, presyncope, disequilibrium, nausea, diplopia, etc. Take the best history you can, but be at peace with the fact that most patients just can’t reliably tell you what they’re feeling (“I don’t know, doc, I just felt off”). Try to sort the rest out with a good neuro exam and the special maneuvers below. If all else fails, all dizzy people over the age of 60, especially with vascular risk factors, should be fully evaluated for an acute neurovascular event.
Your task in the ED is to categorize the patient into one of three groups: 1) Benign Peripheral Positional Vertigo: History: lasts for less than 2 minutes, brought on by head movement
Exam: Dix-Hallpike may produce classic mixed upbeating and torsional nystagmus towards the affected ear that starts
after a delay (10-40 seconds - this takes patience during the exam!), then goes away after 30 seconds (“fatigues”), then the patient may feel spinning in the opposite direction upon sitting up; one side is usually much worse than the other. The response is less severe if you Dix-Hallpike them again (“fatigues”).
Plan: Teach them the Epley, have them follow up with ENT if it doesn’t improve. If it really seems like BPPV without
vertigo/nystagmus on Dix-Hallpike, teach them Epleys anyways; the otolith could also be in the horizontal canal, in which case the test and treatment are different 2) Vestibular system dysfunction (vestibular neuronitis, labyrinthitis): History: lasts for hours to days; labyrinthitis has hearing loss or tinnitus, vestibular neuronitis does not
Exam: Nystagmus is mixed horizontal-torsional away from the affected side. Head thrust (see vestibulocular reflex
below) – vestibular system dysfunction pt breaks fixation
Plan: Fluids, meclizine, steroid taper. May need admission (to medicine preferably) because they will likely continue
to vomit for several more days 3) Central vertigo: History: lasts hours to days, usually has associated symptoms (eg- dysarthria, diplopia, ataxia, weakness, etc)
Exam: Central Nystagmus: Direction changing (eg. to right in right gaze, left in left-gaze), horizontal/rotatory,
pendular, or vertical. There may be other brainstem or cerebellar findings on exam, but the absence of other symptoms cannot exclude a central cause. Use HINTS to INFARCT (sens 100%, spec 96%; Stroke 2009;40:3504) Tests (mnemonic: HINTS) (note, patient must be currently symptomatic for tests to be reliable) 1. Head Impulse – ask the patient to fixate on your nose and move his/her head rapidly 30o off midline and back to center. Look for corrective saccades. Test is normal in central vertigo, abnormal with peripheral vertigo (vestibular nerve dysfunction). 2. Nystagmus - evaluate in primary gaze and horizontal/vertical gaze in all directions. Benign etiologies have unidirectional nystagmus throughout, although the intensity may be different. Central lesions have horizontal direction change (eg left-beating in left gaze, right-beating in right gaze). Vertical nystagmus is always central. 3. Test of Skew – ask the patient to fixate on your nose and then cover/uncover each eye. If positive, you will see vertical corrective saccade/refixation when moving from one eye to the other (horizontal movement doesn’t count) Test interpretation (mnemonic: INFARCT). Concern for stroke if one or more of the following is true: - Impulse Normal - Fast-phase nystagmus Alternating - Refixation on Cover Test
Plan: Admit as a presumed posterior circulation stroke. PICA infarctions can be subtle and can evolve over time. Be
careful with use of MRI to identify these patients (especially in the ED) – hyperacute MRI may appear falsely negative in posterior fossa infarction

title: 1 Neuro-ophthalmologic Emergencies
text:
1 Neuro-ophthalmologic Emergencies
Acute Third Nerve Palsy - Start with a HCT to rule out subarachnoid blood or herniation. - Complete or partial ophthalmoparesis with pupil involvement requires some type of angiography to evaluate for a compressive aneurysm – start with CTA or MRA but then may still need conventional angiogram. You may also want to get MRI instead of/ in addition to CT scan depending on luxuries of time and resource. - Complete opthalmoparesis with spared pupil in patients over 50 with vascular risk factors is likely ischemic palsy, consider getting an MRI scan and then observing. Usually improve within 2-3 months. - Partial opthalmoparesis with spared pupil could be caused by an aneurysm, angiography is needed. - If in doubt with an acute 3rd nerve palsy, image and obtain angiography.
Pituitary Apoplexy Symptoms: Classic syndrome: HA, sudden visual loss (both visual field and acuity), and ophthalmoplegia). May be accompanied by meningeal signs, hypopituitarism, unstable BP, encephalopathy, facial pain/numbness. Differential: Pituitary tumor infarction or hemorrhage and post-partum hemorrhage/Sheehan syndome. Management: CT scan may miss so MRI (pituitary-protocol) is needed. LP may demonstrate subarachnoid blood and aseptic meningitis. If MRI isn’t conclusive and there is SAH, you may have to get angiographic study to rule out aneurysm. Immediate therapy includes. steroids/hydrocortisone to prevent shock from acute hypopituitarism. Electrolyte abnormalities may be prominent and need to be addressed acutely.
Giant Cell Arteritis Symptoms : HA, tenderness of scalp, temple or face of affected side(s), jaw claudication and current or past history of polymyalgia rheumatic, acute vision loss - anterior ischemic optic neuropathy (AION), can also have central retinal artery occlusion, posterior circulation infarction. Usually female (ratio 3:1) and over 60-70 (almost unheard of <50 yrs), may have premonitory symptoms of transient monocular blindness, bright light amaurosis, transient binocular diplopia and formed visual hallucinations. Exam: Look for scalp tenderness, visual acuity loss, chalky white disc edema, and cotton wool spots. If evidence of both central retinal artery occlusion and AION, you have likely sealed the diagnosis. Can have motility defects from ischemia of the extra-ocular muscles, nerves or a brainstem infarct Management – Check ESR - most will be above 40 mm/hr in giant cell arteritis. Consult vascular/ophtho for biopsy. Immediately treat with steroids - IV Solumedrol 1 g daily x 3-5 days then 1mg/kg per day PO prednisone. ***Do not wait for biopsy to start treatment!
Ocular Cranial Nerve Palsies: General rules for localization:

Diplopia resolves upon covering one eye: if it doesn’t the problem is likely ocular or refractive.
On cover- uncover testing, an eye that is deviated out (exotropic) will move in when uncovered to pick up the fixation target (usually a finger or your nose)  Think INO, IIIrd n. palsy or exotropia related to medication effect. If the eyes move out with uncover (esotropia)  think of a sixth nerve palsy or abduction deficit.
Mimics of extraocular nerve palsies: thyroid eye disease, myasthenia gravis, internuclear ophthalmoplegia, decompensated phoria, orbital wall fractures, Wernicke’s syndrome, skew deviation, botulism, orbital tumors, Miller Fisher variant of Guillian-Barre Syndrome, mitochondrial d/o (CPEO: chronic progressive external ophthalmoplegia, Kearns-Sayre Syndrome), vitamin E deficiency

Afferent visual complaints: 1. History: monocular vs. binocular, pain, timing 2. Exam: acuity (best corrected with glasses and pinhole), color (especially red), visual fields (including blind spot), fundus, pupils (is there an APD) 3. Special tests: visual fields (Humphrey, Goldmann), OCT (quantifies retinal nerve fiber layer thickness), orbital ultrasound and the rest of the neurology favorites (MRI, LP etc.)

title: 1 NIHSS and Tools
text:
1 NIHSS and Tools
rTPA Cryoprecipitate 0.1 units/kg iv if fibrinogen <200 mg/dL
If bleeding is refractory or life threatening consider Aminocaproic acid 4-6 gm IV bolus, followed by continuous infusion 1g/hr for 8 h Enteric- Coated Aspirin
Best supportive care (based on PATCH trial) or 1 unit single donor platelet if a surgical procedure is scheduled or bleeding is refractory (*d/w stroke attg)
Can consider repeat 1 single donor unit if PFA- 100 (orderable in EPIC) is not normalized. Consider DDAVP iv or rVIIa** iv if refractory bleeding Clopidogrel (Plavix®)
Best supportive care (based on PATCH trial) or 2 units of single donor platelets if a surgical procedure is scheduled or bleeding is refractory (*d/w stroke attg)
Consider repeating 2 units Q12 hours until the PFA-100 and Verify Now (Plavix inhibition <20% or PRU over 240) assays are normal. Both tests are orderable in EPIC. Consider DDAVP iv or rVIIa** iv if refractory bleeding **Per P&T committee guidelines adult hematology consult required for approval
NIH Stroke Scale 1a. Level of Consciousness 0= Alert; keenly responsive 1= Not alert, but arousable by minor stimulation 2 = Not alert, requires repeated stimulation 3 = Responds only with reflex movements or less 1b. LOC Questions Month & age 0 = Answers both questions correctly 1 = Answers one question correctly 2 = Answers neither question correctly 1c. LOC Commands Open/close eyes Open/close grip
0 = Performs both tasks correctly 1 = Performs one task correctly 2 = Performs neither task correctly 2. Best Gaze 0 = Normal 1 = Partial gaze palsy 2 = Forced deviation, or total gaze paresis 3. Visual 0 = No visual loss 1 = Partial hemianopia 2 = Complete hemianopia 3 = Bilateral hemianopia (blind incl. cortical blindness) 4. Facial Palsy 0 = Normal symmetrical movement 1 = Minor paralysis (asymmetry) 2 = Partial paralysis (lower face) 3 = Complete paralysis (upper and lower face) 5. & 6. Motor Arm & Leg 0 = No drift, limb holds posture for full 10s 1 = Drift, limb holds posture, no drift to bed 2 = Some antigravity effort, cannot maintain posture, drifts down to bed 3 = No effort against gravity, limb falls 4 = No movement 7. Limb Ataxia 0 = Absent 1 = Present in one limb 2 = Present in two limbs 8. Sensory 0 = Normal; no sensory loss 1 = Mild to moderate sensory loss 2 = Severe to total sensory loss 9. Best Language 0 = No aphasia, normal 1 = Mild to moderate aphasia 2 = Severe aphasia 3 = Mute, global aphasia 10. Dysarthria 0 = Normal 1 = Mild to moderate 2 = Severe, unintelligible or mute/anarthric 11. Extinction/Neglect 0 = No abnormality 1 = Extinction to bilateral simultaneous stimulation 2 = Profound neglect
NIHSS Tools From http://www.ninds.nih.gov/doctors/NIH_Stroke_Scale.pdf
You know how.
Down to earth.
I got home from work.
Near the table in the dining room.
They heard him speak on the radio last night.
MAMA TIP-TOP FIFTY-FIFTY THANKS HUCKLEBERRY BASEBALL PLAYER

title: 1 Nonconvulsive Status Epilepticus
text:
1 Nonconvulsive Status Epilepticus
UF NON-CONVULSIVE STATUS EPILEPTICUS PROTOCOL (ADULT)
Immediately:

Order lorazepam STAT
First 5 min give 2mg lorazepam IVP over 2 min
If still seizing, then repeat dose of 1-2 mg to total of 0.1mg/kg
Watch for hypotension and respiratory depression
Order STAT EEG

5-30 min

Order STAT fosphenytoin 20 mg PE/kg at rate of 150 mg PE/min (slow rate if hypotension occurs) Maximum dose = 2000 mg
Order Free phenytoin level 2 hours after load. Goal free PHT level of 2-3
If level at goal , start maintenance fosphenytoin 5 mg PE/kg in 3 divided doses per day
If level not at goal, redose using calculation: 0.8 x (weight in kg) x (desired
actual level) x10 = mini-load in mg
can round to nearest 250 mg
Monitor for hypotension and cardiac arrhythmias

By 30 min - Call NeuroCCM fellow phone (352-745-1912) for NICU bed - Convert STAT EEG to Long term video EEG monitoring - Call Epilepsy attending and Neurocritical Care team for further management recommendations and discussion - Options: - Valproic acid: Load IV 40mg/kg; Maintenance: 30 mg/kg/day divided every 8 hours (max dose 60 mg/kg/day) Avoid use with liver failure - Lacosamide 400mg bolus ; Maintenance dose: 200 mg IV q 12 hours (max dose 600 mg daily) Monitor for PR prolongation - Levetiracetam 60 mg/kg loading dose; Maintenance dose: 1000 mg IV every 12 hours (max dose 12,000 mg daily) - Phenobarbital total loading dose of 20 mg/kg, divided into smaller 5 mg/kg loading doses at a rate of 10 mg per minute, repeated every two hours until total load given (can be given in non-intubated patient but not preferred, reduced risk of hypotension/ respiratory suppression, typical rate for convulsive status in intubated patient is 60 mg per minute) Monitor for respiratory depression, hypotension - If nonconvulsive seizures continue, intubate and load with midazolam 0.2 mg/kg IV bolus, may repeat bolus dose after 5 minutes if seizures not controlled. Start maintenance dose at 0.1 mg/kg/hr, may increase by 0.1 mg/kg/hr every 20-30 minutes until seizures controlled (usual dose 0.2-0.4 mg/kg/hr; max dose 2.9 mg/kg/hr) monitor for hypotension (patient must be intubated) - If still having nonconvulsive seizures: consider pentobarbital coma (MUST be ordered by CCM) in consultation with Epilepsy attending and General Neurology attending with further management to be determined on a case by case basis
Important Practice Points

Make sure ED weighs patient on arrival; this protocol is contingent on WEIGHT BASED DOSING.
Make sure that timing of doses, infusion rate and actual doses are accurate prior to order (see EPIC status epilepticus order set once available)
If fosphenytoin is not available, can use equivalent dose of phenytoin

title: 1 Optic Neuritis and Transverse Myelitis
text:
1 Optic Neuritis and Transverse Myelitis
OPTIC NEURITIS
Epi: usually 20s-40s, 2:1 F:M Presentation: monocular vison loss/scotoma, red desat, painful eye movements (90%), Uhtoff’s Exam: swollen disks in 1/3 only, central vision loss, poor color plates/red hatpin, +APD
Workup + Admission + Treatment: 1) Blood tests, CXR, and LP are not helpful (unless atypical ON); order MRI for prediction of MS 2) Consider formal VF testing (Goldmann or Humphrey). Consider NMO testing since treatment of this condition is different from routine optic neuritis. 3) Admit for IV Solumedrol 1g/d x 3-5 days, expect recovery in 2-3 weeks 4) Plan to start immunomodulatory therapy if MRI with >2 lesions (>3mm diam, perivent or ovoid) to reduce risk of MS: Interferon beta-1a (Avonex) group had 44% less risk of CDMS at 3 years (CHAMPS trial, Jacobs et al, 2000); interferon beta-1a (Rebif) group had 34% probability of conversion to MS at 2 years (compared to 45% of placebo; ETOMs trial, Comi et al, 2001); interferon beta-1b group had 28% probability of conversion to MS at 2 years (compared to 45% of placebo; BENEFIT trial, Kappos et al, 2006)
Evidence for treatment in acute optic neuritis (Optic Neuritis Treatment Trial, 1992,1993,1997) - IV steroids - speed vision recovery, no change in acuity vs. placebo at 5 yrs - IV steroids - 50% less develop CDMS at 2 yrs (8% vs 17%), but no difference at 5 years - Oral steroids - increased recurrent ON vs IV/placebo (41% vs 25%) over 5 yrs (don’t treat acute ON with oral steroids)
ACUTE TRANSVERSE MYELITIS 1. Demyelinating: NMOSD: Dx Criteria: Core Clinical Characteristics: Optic neuritis, acute myelitis, Area postrema syndrome, Acute Brainstem syndrome, Acute diencephalic syndrome Additional MRI requirements: for ON: Optic Nerve MRI inflammation >1/2 optic nerve. For myelitis: cord lesion > 3 segments, MRI brain not c/w MS, NMO-IgG positive (sen 58-76%, sp 85-99%) NMOSD with AQP4-IgG: At least 1 core clinical; Positive AQP4-IgG NMOSD with negative AQP-IgG: At least 2 core clinical (at least 1 has to be ON or TM); Dissemination in space; MRI requirements as applicable

Distinguish from MS: long cord lesions, usually nl MRI brain (atypical when they occur), CSF can have cells (PMN), neg CSF OCBs, NMO Abs rare in MS Treatment: Pheresis (acute); Rituximab (prophylaxis) at 375mg/m2 weekly x 4 weeks (Wingerchuk, et al Neurology 2015:85:1-13)


Infectious: Dx Testing should include HSV-1,2; EBV, VZV, lyme, HTLV-1, HHV-6, CMV, WNV
Misc inflammatory: SLE, Sjogren’s, MCTD, Bechet’s, Sarcoidosis
Nutritional: B12, folate, copper, vitamin E

Approach: 1. MRI GAD  R/o Compression (if compression, call a surgeon) 2. If > 3 segments, consider NMO, SLE, Cu def, sarcoid, infarcts, infection * If pleocytosis (> 20-50 cells), check HSV, VZV, Lyme, EBV, HHV-6, HTLV-1, Enterovirus, WNV * Serologic testing should include: ANA, SSA/B, Lyme, Anti-cardiolipin Ab & Anti-phopholipid Abs, HTLV-1, HIV-1, RPR, B12, folate, Copper * More ancillary testing: Chest CT (sarcoid), Lip Biopsy (Sjogrens), Brain/Meningeal Biopsy
CSF inflammation and its mimics = inflammogram - CSF: oligoclonal bands, myelin basic protein, VDRL, JC virus PCR (if immunosuppressed) - Serum: SPEP, ANA, ANCA, SSA/B, ACE, RPR, Lyme ab, HIV, B12, ESR, TSH

title: 1 Seizure Consult
text:
1 Seizure Consult
HISTORY: New onset seizure or known epilepsy?
If known epilepsy: goal is to determine AED regimen and compliance, seizure type/frequency and last seizure, epilepsy physician, any recent triggers (see below). Check AED levels and if low or they are non-compliant, re- load; if not, consider increasing AED dose. Ask about prior AEDs tried.
If new onset seizures, obtain comprehensive first seizure history: - RISK FACTORS: history of febrile seizures, family history of seizures, prenatal and perinatal complications, developmental delay, history of head trauma, history of CNS infection, history of CNS mass/malformation, metabolic disorder, stroke (hemorrhagic>ischemic), drug/alcohol use, consanguinity - TRIGGERS LOWERING SEIZURE THRESHOLD: Medications, AED noncompliance, infection, metabolic abnormalities, toxic ingestion, menses, stress, sleep deprivation, EtOH/benzo withdrawal, fever, use of stimulants, hypoglycemia, electrolyte disturbance, hypoxia, acute medical/neuro illness - CHARACTERISTICS: aura (visual, auditory, olfactory, epigastric rising sensation, psychic (déjà vu, derealization, depersonalization, fear, anxiety), type of seizure (especially how its starts), eyes open or closed, lateral eye or head deviation, length of seizure, associated tongue bite or other injury, incontinence, post-ictal confusion, post-ictal weakness. - SUBTLE SEIZURE SIGNS: history of staring, automatisms, waking up incontinent or injured (tongue bite), myoclonic jerks.
EXAM: Focus on mental status, post-ictal paralysis or other focal deficits, subtle signs of ongoing seizures such as abnormal movements, head or eye deviation, eyelid twitching, pupillary abnormalities or hippus; tongue bite/other injury, and meningeal signs
ED WORKUP: AED levels (only carbamazepine, phenytoin, valproate and phenobarb levels are available acutely, but others can be helpful to establish compliance), non-contrast head CT (new seizures only!), can consider MRI with/without contrast (seizure protocol). Basic labs including electrolytes and CBC, infectious workup (UA, CXR), toxicology screen, LP if concern for meningitis
PLAN: - AED: load and then start maintenance dose (do not start AEDs for an uncomplicated first seizure) - EEG: highest yield within 24-48 h. If the patient is not “back to baseline,” consider stat EEG. - MRI with and without contrast, seizure protocol if warranted - Ativan IV PRN seizure activity - Evaluate medication list for medications that lower the seizure threshold - Can be discharged from the ED if they only had 1-2 seizure, have returned to baseline, and have nothing in the above workup requiring admission. - Information patient that he/she cannot drive for ≥ 6 months per Florida state law - Refer to the Urgent Clinic follow-up or your continuity clinic via Epic.
Not Returning to Baseline after a Seizure The two most common reasons patients don’t wake up are prolonged post-ictal periods and overly eager benzo administration … But don’t miss anything important: - Make sure the patient isn’t still seizing. Look for subtle abnormal limb or eye movement, fluctuating arousal or complete lack of improvement. 40% of patients with status epilepticus continue having subclinical seizures after convulsive seizures are controlled. Consider a stat EEG. - Get a head CT if you don’t have one yet - Consider LP if you have any concern for meningitis/encephalitis or SAH not seen on HCT - You may need to admit a patient overnight for prolonged postical period or benzo intoxication. If there is no medical reason they had a seizure (e.g. EtOH w/d, infection) they will likely need to be admitted to neurology.

title: 1 Stroke Admission Orders
text:
1 Stroke Admission Orders
and Workup: exceptions to guidelines if tPA given - For routine stroke admissions, use order set “NEU STROKE PROGRAM ISCHEMIC STROKE ADMISSION” Post-tPA or mechanical thrombectomy patients use order set “NEU STROKE PROGRAM ORDERS – POST ALTEPLASE (T-PA) ADMISSION ORDERS IP UF” - Bedside dysphagia screen performed by nursing, must be documented in chart. If the patient fails, he/she MUST have formal swallow evaluation. - Patient who fail dysphagia screen must be strict NPO (this includes oral medications!) until they are cleared by speech/swallow - Make sure any medication orders accurately reflect the patient’s po status (eg oral or DHT route) - ASA 325 mg PO if swallowing, 300 mg PR if not (unless on anticoagulation or tPA in last 24 hours) - Statin - NS @ 100 cc/hr (careful with patients with CHF or on HD). Avoid hypotonic fluids. - acetaminophen 650 mg q4h prn temp >100.4 - sliding scale insulin and fingersticks q6h - neurochecks q4h on floor (q2h in IMC, q1h in NICU) - UA/UDS - CXR - EKG in ED, telemetry/monitoring after admission - SCD (all) plus Heparin SQ 5,000 units q8h (unless tPA in last 24 hours) - Permissive hypertension (SBP<220, DBP<110), hold home BP meds (except ½ dose β-blocker) o Exception: BP goal will be lower following IV tPA (<180/105) or mechanical thrombectomy (goal depends on procedure result, discuss with fellow/attending) - Labs: lipid panel, HgbA1c, RPR, liver function tests, troponin, TSH, folate/B12 - If concerned for endocarditis, send serial blood cultures - Transthoracic echocardiogram (+/- bubble study if <60 years old) - MRI brain - MRA head and neck vs. carotid ultrasound/transcranial dopplers if patient has not had CTA head/neck **Certain patients (e.g., younger patients) may require TEE, hypercoagulable or vasculitis workup. Discuss with attending before ordering. Hypercoagulable panel: Antiphospholipid panel (anticardiolipin, DRVVT, beta-2 microglobulin, prothrombin gene mutation, Factor V Leiden mutation, Protein C activity, Protein S activity, antithrombin III activity, HIT antibody (in appropriate clinical setting).

title: 1 Stroke Alert
text:
1 Stroke Alert
ALL ALERTS MUST BE DISCUSSED WITH STROKE ATTENDING/FELLOW IN REAL TIME
Joint Commission goals: - Door to neuro eval: <15 min - Door to CT completion: <20 min - CT complete to interpretation: <20 min (*use the time stamp in Visage if you interpreted in real time) - Door to IV tPA: <30 min - Door to groin puncture (thrombectomy): <60 min
Time guidelines: Intravenous tPA: < 4.5 hours of symptom onset Mechanical thrombectomy: < 6h in most cases, potentially up to 24h depending on perfusion scan
Initial steps: 1. When was the patient last known normal? Don’t trust anybody - FIND OUT YOURSELF. Obtain family phone contact information from EMS in case needed for timeline confirmation and eventual consent. 2. BRIEF medical history (similar sx in past, acuity of onset, fluctuation/progression of sx, known stroke causes [Afib, carotid disease], associated sx (headache/neck pain, altered consciousness, abnormal movements), current antithrombotics 3. Check blood pressure 4. Fingerstick glucose. Correct if <50 or >400. If patient is still symptomatic, proceed with acute vascular workup 5. Rapid neuro exam/NIHSS – do NOT delay CT/IV/blood draw. Learn to work around ED providers. 6. Take patient to CT scan as soon as possible. - UF stroke alert protocol is non-contrast HCT, CTA head/neck, and CT perfusion - Non-contrast HCT alone is sufficient for tPA decision. If the perfusion scanner is down, do NOT waste time taking the patient to a different tower. - Stay with the patient and immediately review the scan yourself. Do NOT wait for CTA to begin to mobilize for IV tPA. CTA/CTP requires post-processing and is NOT necessary for IV tPA. 7. Review tPA inclusion/exclusion criteria to confirm patient is eligible 8. Call stroke attending/fellow as soon as possible to discuss treatment plan. 9. Review risks/benefits with patient and/or family. Obtain verbal consent if possible. See below for suggested language.* If patient cannot provide consent and family is not immediately available by phone, two attendings (stroke and ED) can provide emergency consent. Do not waste time making more than 1-2 attempts to contact family. 10. If IV tPA is planned: - Tenectaplase for all patients - Inform nurse/pharmacist/ED team of plan - Get weight in kilograms (if not obtained on arrival) - Order tPA in Epic. TNK (single bolus): 0.25 mg/kg; max dose 25 mg - Ensure patient has 2 well-functioning IVs, preferably 20-gauge or larger - Make sure BP is <185/110 before pushing tPA bolus** - Remain with the patient at bedside until tPA is administered to ensure there is no delay. 11. Consider patient for mechanical thrombectomy (see below) and/or acute stroke clinical trial.
*Suggested language for consent: “You are having an acute stroke, caused by a blood clot blocking one of the arteries in your brain. The symptoms you’re currently having may become permanent if we don’t act quickly. You are eligible for a medication called tPA, which will help dissolve the blood clot. Studies in thousands of patients with strokes like yours have shown that with this medication, patients have a much better chance of recovering to normal and living at home after a stroke. Because this medication temporarily thins your blood, there is a possible risk of bleeding, including bleeding in the brain. However, the potential benefit from the treatment is 6–10 times greater than the risks of harm, and I believe this medication is the best chance we have of reversing your symptoms. The faster we start the treatment, the better your chances of recovery. Do we have your permission to proceed?” **If BP elevated: 1) Labetalol 10-20mg IV over 1-2 minutes, may repeat x 1 OR 2) Start Nicardipine gtt at 5mg/hr IV, titrate up by 2.5mg/hr at 5-15 minute intervals, max dose: 15mg/hr. Can consider clevidipine gtt. - If BP does not improve despite these interventions, then discuss eligibility further with attending/fellow.
IV tPA Inclusion Criteria: - Clinical diagnosis of stroke with neurological deficit that would be considered disabling - Established time of symptom onset/last seen normal within past 4.5 hours Potential tPA exclusions to consider: (Note: many of these rules are flexible in the correct clinical scenario, therefore each case MUST be discussed expeditiously with stroke attending/fellow) - Significant head trauma or prior stroke in previous 3 months - Symptoms suggestive of subarachnoid hemorrhage - Arterial puncture at noncompressible site in previous 7 days - History of previous intracranial hemorrhage 1. Intracranial neoplasm, aneurysm, or arteriovenous malformation 2. Recent intracranial or intraspinal surgery 3. SBP > 185, DBP > 110 at time of treatment despite appropriate therapy 4. Active internal bleeding 5. Platelets <100K 6. Heparin received within 48 hours with elevated PTT > 40 sec 7. Current use of anticoagulant with INR > 1.7 or PT > 15 sec 8. Current use of direct thrombin inhibitors or factor Xa inhibitors 9. CT demonstrates large lobar infarction (hypodensity > 1/3 cerebral hemisphere) 10. Blood glucose concentration < 50mg/dL (correct first but treat if concern for stroke) 11. Minor or rapidly improving stroke symptoms (ensure residual is not disabling) 12. Pregnancy 13. Seizure at onset with postictal residual neurological impairments 14. Major surgery or serious trauma within previous 14 days 15. Recent GI or GU tract hemorrhage within previous 21 days 16. Acute MI within previous 3 months * In patients without history of thrombocytopenia, IV tPA can be initiated before availability of platelet count, but should be discontinued if plt < 100k ** In patients without recent use of oral anticoagulants or heparin, treatment with tPA can be initiated before availability of coagulation test results but should be discontinued if INR > 1. 7. *** Caution if recent hemodialysis with administration of heparin bolus
Mechanical thrombectomy Consideration for mechanical thrombectomy should begin immediately after tPA decision is made. Eligibility varies depending on time from symptom onset/last known well 1) Symptom onset <6h: requires large-vessel occlusion on CTA (or suspected based on clinical exam), NIHSS ≥ 6, ASPECTS (determined from non-contrast HCT) ≥ 6. Do NOT wait for CTP results per AHA guidelines. 2) Symptom onset 6-24 hours: large-vessel occlusion on CTA, CTP suggestive of large penumbra/small core meeting criteria from DAWN/DEFUSE-3 trials. Hyperacute-MRI protocol can be considered in select patients who are unable to tolerate contrast (discuss with attending). Review expeditiously with the stroke fellow/attending. If the patient is appropriate for intervention, the stroke attending will call the endovascular neurosurgery attending directly. - Call ShandsCair (#50300 on house phone) or #69 (inpatient stroke alerts) to request a STROKE ENDOVASCULAR THERAPY ALERT. Dispatch will page out the following stroke alert: “[Patient room number/MRN] confirmed for endovascular therapy” - Page neurosurgery consult resident (413-4736 or 413-2125) to inform that patient has been accepted for intervention by endovascular. Also call the IR tech to notify of an incoming case (352-256-5402). -Accompany the patient to the OR to provide in-person signout to the anesthesia team. -Place admission orders ASAP (see below) so that the patient can leave the OR expeditiously -Accompany the patient to the NICU for signout and to perform/document a post-procedure NIHSS. Orders after IV tPA treatment and/or mechanical thrombectomy (use order set “NEU STROKE PROGRAM ORDERS - POST ALTEPLASE (T-PA) ADMISSION ORDERS IP UF”): - Admit to NICU (call CCM fellow to notify) - Monitor BP (maintain <180/105 post tPA, discuss goal BP with attending for mechanical thrombectomy): vitals q15 min x 2 h after start, then q30 min x 6 h, then q1 h x 16 h - No heparin, warfarin, aspirin, other antiplatelets/anticoagulation for 24 hours if pt received tPA - Avoid invasive procedures (eg, no NG tubes, no Foley) and arterial access at non-compressible sites for 24 h if patient received tPA - Repeat CT scan 24 hours post tPA to exclude hemorrhage – if negative can give ASA 325 mg, start DVT prophylaxis

title: 1 Subarachnoid Hemorrhage
text:
1 Subarachnoid Hemorrhage
Thunderclap headache ddx SAH, pituitary apoplexy, arterial dissection, venous sinus thrombosis, hypertensive urgency, RCVS, intracranial hypotension, primary thunderclap headache Sulcal subarachnoid hemorrhage ddx RCVS, venous sinus thrombosis, vasculitis, mycotic aneurysm, trauma, amyloid angiopathy, meningeal neoplasm, dural AV fistula, meningitis
Immediate Management in the ED To conform the diagnosis: - Non-contrast CT - LP if CT findings are normal (xanthochromia might be falsely negative if <6h from symptom onset)
Follow up Management after confirmed SAH

Obtain CBC, electrolytes, Ca, Mg, PT/PTT, glucose
ECG and CXR to rule out neurogenic pulmonary edema or cardiac stunning
Keep SBP <160 mm Hg until aneurysm secured. Use labetalol 10 mg IV if HR >60, hydralazine 10 mg IV for HR <60
Neurosurgery consultation for angiography and/or EVD
EVD if patient has depressed LOC and hydrocephalus
Tranexamic acid 1 g IV if onset within past 72 hours; caution in patients with severe coronary artery
disease or who have a delayed presentation and may already be in vasospasm
Monitor for complications: rebleeding, hydrocephalus, vasospasm and hyponatremia due to cerebral salt wasting or SIADH
Call NICU consultant, admit to ICU
Bedside dysphagia evaluation to be performed by admitting resident. NPO if fails o Consider formal SLP assessment if facial weakness/significant dysarthria o No oral meds prior to bedside dysphagia screen

Admission Orders 1. Include WFNS, Modified Fisher, Hunt and Hess scores in initial note (See tables below) 2. Admit to NICU unit 47 under neurosurgery service 3. Neuro checks per unit routine 4. Order STAT head CT if neurologic deterioration. 5. Blood pressure management: Labetalol 10 mg, every 15 minutes if HR >60, alternatively use hydralazine 10 mg for HR <60. For refractory HTN, initiate nicardipine infusion and titrate to effect. a. Unsecured aneurysm, keep SBP <160 mm Hg b. Consider inserting arterial line for dynamic monitoring 6. Continue TXA 1g q6h until aneurysm is secured (no more than 72h); caution in pts with severe cardiac disease 7. Nimodipine 60 mg every 4 hours by mouth or NG tube for 21 days for delayed cerebral ischemia prophylaxis. Transition to 30 mg q2h if patient develops hypotension with typical dosing. 8. Monitor for elevated intracranial pressure 9. Discuss management with NCCM fellow and neurosurgery 10. Strict bedrest and strict I/Os, insert urinary catheter 11. Daily electrolytes to monitor for hyponatremia

title: 1 TBI
text:
1 TBI
protocol
Paroxysmal Sympathetic Hyperactivity (PSH)
PSH is a disorder in the regulation of autonomic function with acute brain injury, most notably severe TBI - Recurrent episodes of excessive sympathetic activity that start rapidly, are often induced by stimulation, and resolve spontaneously after a variable period of time or after administration of an abortive medication - Core clinical features include tachycardia, hypertension, tachypnea, hyperthermia, sweating, and/or increased muscle tone with possible dystonic posturing - Associated conditions —brain trauma, nontraumatic forms of acute brain disease, including anoxic-ischemic coma after cardiac arrest, intracranial hemorrhage, autoimmune encephalitis, ischemic stroke, hydrocephalus, and cerebral fat embolism syndrome

title: 1 TIA Risk Stratification and Stroke Mechanisms
text:
1 TIA Risk Stratification and Stroke Mechanisms
TIAs: As a general rule of thumb, all TIA patients should be observed for 24 hours and evaluated expeditiously for high- risk stroke mechanism. If they want to go home, tell them the risk: The ABCD2 score predicts risk of stroke within 2 days of a TIA (Lancet 369: 282-292, 2007): Age ≥60 years [1 point] Blood pressure ≥140/90 mm Hg [1 point] Clinical features: unilateral weakness [2 points], speech impairment without weakness [1 point] Duration ≥60 min [2 points] or 10–59 min [1 point] Diabetes [1 point] Score 6–7 = High risk = 8.1% 2-day risk 4–5 = Moderate risk = 4.1% 0–3 = Low risk = 1.0%
Stroke mechanisms: Most common mechanisms of stroke include: - Cardioembolism (~20% ischemic stroke): Atrial fibrillation (most common, esp in older adults), CHF/CM with low EF, LV or LAA thrombus, cardiac tumor (myxoma/fibroelastoma), mechanical valve, endocarditis (infectious or marantic [autoimmune/malignancy]), paradoxical embolism (PFO, pulm AVM) - Large-artery atherosclerosis (~15%) – extracranial or intracranial (affects treatment choice) - Small-vessel disease (~25%) – must be subcortical/brainstem/cerebellum. Small cortical strokes are generally embolic. Lacunar strokes are usually linked to vascular risk factors, esp uncontrolled DM and tobacco - Rare causes (<5%) – see below, also consider hypercoagulable state from undiagnosed malignancy - Cryptogenic stroke (~20-30%) – no cause found despite thorough workup. If embolic-appearing, may be ESUS (embolic stroke of undetermined source), consider long-term Afib monitoring, no role for empiric AC per ESUS trials to date.
Young Stroke: In addition to conventional stroke mechanisms, consider the following for patients <60 years old: Cardiac disease (including congenital, rheumatic valve disease, mitral valve prolapse, patent foramen ovale, endocarditis, atrial myxoma, arrhythmias, cardiac surgery) Large vessel disease  Premature atherosclerosis – consider radiation vasculopathy in patients with previous head/neck irradiation. Also consider HIV  Dissection (spontaneous or traumatic)  Inherited metabolic diseases (homocystinuria, Fabry’s, pseudoxanthoma elasticum, MELAS syndrome)  Fibromuscular dysplasia  Infection (bacterial, fungal, tuberculosis, syphilis, Lyme)  Vasculitis (collagen vascular diseases — systemic lupus erythematosus, rheumatoid arthritis, Sjögren’s syndrome, polyarteritis nodosa; Takayasu’s disease, Wegener’s syndrome, cryoglobulinemia, sarcoidosis, inflammatory bowel disease, isolated central nervous system angiitis)  Moyamoya disease  Toxic (illicit drugs — cocaine, heroin, phencyclidine; therapeutic drugs — L-asparaginase, cytosine arabinoside) Small vessel disease  Vasculopathy (infectious, noninfectious, microangiopathy) Hematologic disease  Sickle-cell disease, leukemia, thrombocytosis, polycythemia vera  Hypercoagulable states (antiphospholipid antibody syndromes, deficiency of antithrombin III or protein S or C, resistance to activated protein C, increased factor VIII, MTHFR)  DIC, TTP  Venous occlusion (dehydration, parameningeal infection, meningitis, neoplasm, polycythemia, leukemia, inflammatory bowel disease)

title: 1 VA Stroke Alert
text:
1 VA Stroke Alert
S with concern for large vessel occlusion
Evaluate patient emergently and obtain noncontrast head CT as you would normally. Do not give contrast (i.e. CTA) at VA if concern for large vessel occlusion, as availability at the VA will introduce a time delay. You can give tPA at the VA after discussion with attending if indicated.
If concern for large vessel stroke that may be amenable to endovascular intervention, bring this to the attention of VA attending immediately. If the VA attending agrees to transfer to UF, he/she must call the Shands transfer center (352-265-0559) to speak with the on-call stroke attending - do NOT bypass this step by calling the stroke attending directly. Once the patient has been accepted for transfer, follow the steps below.* If patient is within tPA window do not delay tPA administration, you can give bolus and start infusion while setting up the transfer.** Make sure nurse continues to monitor blood pressure with tPA infusion.
To transfer to UF: Regular Hours (8-4:30p, M-F) 1. Call VA transfer coordinator (Teresa Taylor @ 352-264-3848 or 352-339-9126 or Sharonda Taylor- Martin @ 386-487-1750 ext 1788) and inform them of need for emergent transfer 2. Place a Non-VA Care (NVCC) Consult (CPRSConsults (order menu)Non VA Care 3. Complete BENEFICIARY TRAVEL (BT) note template (Notes section of CPRS). After completing the template, an order menu will open, select “GNV Travel IP” option and complete the travel consult order. 4. Obtain consent using iMed Consent. 5. Enter discharge order (In the Discharge Order dialogue box, next to “Discharge Patient” type in “Hospital to Hospital Transfer to Shands pending bed availability.” 6. Write a consult note/DC summary
Afterhours/ weekends/holidays AOD will act as the VA Transfer Coordinator and may be reached at 78-6724 or 78-6004, but otherwise the process will be the same as during regular hours.
*Make resident on service and/or vascular fellow at UF aware of transfer as well. **Stay with patient until ambulance arrives to transfer to UF.
Lake City VA telephone requests Treatment recommendations can only be given for patients whom you have personally evaluated. If you receive a request for telephone consultation from the Lake City VA, the following are the only two available options: - If the description sounds like a potential acute stroke within a therapeutic treatment window, you should advise the physician to contact the VA National Tele-Stroke program from their location or immediately transfer the patient to the nearest Comprehensive Stroke Center of their choice. - If the physician calling you describes ANYTHING else, please state the following verbatim: “As a resident in training, I am not permitted by my Service Chief and Program Directors to provide consultation over the telephone. If your patient needs urgent Neurological Consultation, I will be happy to evaluate the patient if he or she is transferred to this hospital or ED. If your patient does not need urgent Neurological Consultation, please submit a Consult or E-Consult request to LKC or GNV Neurology.”
